definition: To have a bite taken out, to be bitten off. In Mueller circles, used almost exclusively to denote a Rook card with a bent or missing corner (âHeâs got the *abgebiÃene* red 14!â)

An expression of exasperation roughly equivalent to âOh, for Peteâs sake.â Literally something like âOh, you lovely sack of straw [straw mattress],â it may originally have connoted laziness, a fondness for sleeping late, or a habit of dozing off in the afternoon.

When grown-ups would announce a trip to Bemidji for supplies â thereby ruining a dayâs fishing and swimming in Lake George â staying behind was not an option. At least one kid would develop the âBemidji Bock,â a persistent, dark, and very foul mood (see *Bock*, below).

The ceremonial presentation and exchange of Christmas presents. In the Nickel household on Ridgeland Avenue, *Bescherung* began with a ceremonial procession from upstairs down to the living room while singing *Von Himmel Hoch,* but in English, with H.C. at the piano. It was the childrenâs first glimpse of the decorated Christmas tree.
A red nose, an inflamation thought to be caused by overconsumption of alcohol and strong beer. Doctors now call it rosacea.
To commit a large faux pas, make a fool of oneself. Often used as a cognate in English: âHe tried to propose a toast but he blamiered himself.â

The words have several meanings in modern German, but the Muellers were interested in the ones that derive from âbilly-goat, bridge strut, pig-headed person.â To âhave the *Bock*â was to be *bockig* â stubborn, obstinate, sulky, crabby, and determinedly, persistently so.

A boy who had the *Bock* was a *Bockhans* and was given wide berth for the day. A skillful *Bockhans* â and the Muellers produced some great ones â would resist attempts to cheer him up and could remain *bockig* until dinnertime. Used of adults, the term connoted a stern, inflexible demeanor. Adelheid Nickel, who knew a *Bockhans* when she met one, occasionally used the term to describe a dour, obdurate, strict or unusually disputatious Lutheran clergyman. Germans could personify characteristics by adding -Hans or -Peter to some sort of condition (see *BÃ¼pferhans* and *Streuwwelpeter*).

pronunciation: broom â same vowel sound as in âRookâ
definition: A deep or low sound (e.g., the *BrummbaÃ* is the lowest rank of pipes on an organ). The Muellers used this as an onomatopoeic term for flatulence; a fart.
A person â not always a child â who is frequently and loudly flatulent.

A bump, hump, or protuberance. Used as a genial way to describe the physiology of aging. (âHeâs getting a *Buckel*â denoted stoop-shouldered posture.) The word was almost always mispronounced and misspelled by Mueller descendants, who began the word with a P.
The urine in a wet bed; the persistent odor of a wet bed even after the bed has dried.
A wet bed (combined form of *Büpfer* and *Strohsack,* a straw mattress).

A little boy who wets his bed. Used benignly by parents of small children, it was also a powerful teasing word among siblings, even in their adult lives. There was no feminine counterpart. A little girl was never a *Büpferhans,* but she could have a *Büpfersack.*

A glass of water. The archive offers no explanation for the idiom, but it was fairly well known and often used among the Muellers of Freedom Township, Minnesota. Choosing to drink a glass of Charlie Spatzâs beer made a dramatic difference when water was the only available thirst quencher. The phrase had overtones of making the best of an unsatisfactory situation (e.g, being out of beer). It probably derives from *SpaÃ/spaÃen,* a joke/to make a joke out of something. Opa Mueller, guest preacher at a *Missionsfest,* was desperately thirsty while delivering his sermon and slaked his thirst with a huge tankard of water immediately after the benediction ... moments before the host brought out plenty of ice-cold German beer. Opaâs famous quote: *âSchade um den schÃ¶nen Durst!â* (What a shame to have wasted such a beautiful thirst!)
Dunce, dolt; literally a stupid jackass. An expression of frustration sometimes used of oneself, spoken aloud after doing or witnessing something stupid (*âAch, du dummer Esel!â*)

Nonsense, rubbish, a stupid thing or utterance. Used of oneself after doing or saying something stupid or of an inanimate object (as, for example, when insulting a hammer after having whacked oneâs own thumb).

A stupid, foolish person. The word does not occur in the Cassels dictionary, although *Patsch* itself is there (a slapping sound as in smacking oneâs forehead and uttering âduhâ). The word is likely a combination of *dumm* (stupid) and a German cognate of âpatchâ in the obsolete Shakespearean sense (fool, clown, rogue, knave). It was a very common, though somewhat gentle putdown, along the lines of âdummyâ or âdunce.â The Muellers used the term at least four generations into the arrival in America.
To eat like an animal â rapidly, loudly, without manners, until everything is devoured. Humans *essen*; animals *fressen*. Used colloquially at moments of great hunger: âLetâs *fress*.â

To handle dexterously, make rapid or cunning movements. This may be the word from which the word *fuscher* derived. Adelheid Nickel loved to fuscher in forests and meadows, quickly digging up plants of interest and hauling them off to her yard or hunting for heart-shaped rocks. *Fuscher* always had clandestine overtones.

Absolutely dreadful, horrible, ghastly, atrocious. Uttered in exasperation when oneâs situation has taken a turn for the worse or events are overwhelming. Also used in an aesthetic sense for condemnation of poor color choice, doggerel poetry, hymns sung loudly and badly off key, soup that tastes like dishwater, general ugliness.

To ponder, brood, meditate, reflect, muse, ruminate, or be lost in thought. Sometimes used as a cognate in English, as in âI was sitting in church, *griebeling* during the sermon.â The noun form, **GrÃ¼belei** [GREE bul eye], is a blanket term for rumination, brooding, reflection, day-dreaming, and all the other products of *griebeling*.

Entrails, fish guts. Not technically a German phrase, âguts der zytensâ was an earnest childâs coinage by Jimmy Nickel, based on the opening of a common German table prayer he heard at his Uncle Ernstâs farm. The Marquardts said, *âGott sei Dankâ*; Jimmy parsed that to âGuts der zytens.â The full prayer: *Gott sei Dank / fÃ¼r Speisâ und Trank / durch Jesum Christum. Amen*

Misery, distress, woe, lamentation. *Es ist allhier ein Jammertal* (We live in a vale of tears) shows up in Heinrich SchÃ¼tzâs *Musikalische Exequien* (No. 14). In Mueller usage, it was most frequently an expression of exasperation: *Ach Jammer!* â âOh woe is me!â or âI canât stand this anymore!â Also used by parents of unhappy, whining, over-tired kids: âNo more jammering!â

The word does not appear in Cassellâs, but was a frequent part of Mueller conversation. A *Kaplier* was an awkward social situation or a difficult problem. Having 15 dinner guests with food for 10 would be a *Kaplier.* Frau Erica herself famously ran out of coffee at a dinner party â a *Kaplier* at dessert time â but brazened it out by taking an empty coffee pot and faking her way all around the table (âAre you *sure* you wouldnât like another cup?â). Irma Marquardt Gelhausen once remarked that *Kapliers* were mostly a Mueller phenomenon. (See also *Pech.*) A 21st-century coinage uses *Kaplier* as a golf score, representing a triple bogey or worse, as in, âI started the front nine with three *Kapliers* and a par.â

Caterwauling; literally âcats music.â A coinage by H.C. Nickel to describe the 12-tone serial compositions of Arnold SchÃ¶nberg and others. H.C., who knew the German texts and loved the melodies of the Bach chorales, considered Alban Bergâs *Violin Concerto* tantamount to sacrilege because its final movement quotes the chorale tune *âEs Ist Genugâ* from Bachâs *O Ewigkeit, du Donnerwort* (BWV 60). When his son James, then a college student, would play his recording of the Berg, H.C. would leave the room.
A group of children; the kids. Germans often append *Schar* (group, band, horde, flock) to create collective nouns. *Engel* is an angel; *Engelschar* is the multitude of the heavenly host.

To jingle (of coins) or plonk (of a piano). Used exclusively as a cognate because there is no single English verb that connotes the pointless, time-wasting plunking out of notes at a piano to no apparent musical purpose: âJimmy, stop klimpering and practice your scales.â
A log of wood, a lump of something; a clumsy or boorish blockhead; [*adj.*] massive, clumsy, bulky.

Literally a log attached to oneâs leg, similar to a millstone around oneâs neck or a ball and chain. Used to describe a socially awkward, third-wheel person, someone who is part of a group but fails to grasp the spirit of the group, the black hole of fun. Muellers were greatly afraid of being dismissed as a *Klotz am Bein.*

To nibble, gnaw, crunch, or munch, as a dog with a bone or an old shoe. Also used while making a mild rebuke for too much noise while eating crunchy things or for eating, say, a stalk of celery or a raw carrot. âStop *knabbering* on the peanut brittle!â (See *schmatzen*)

Cartilage, gristle. Usually used of chicken or other foodstuff. If there were not enough good pieces of chicken to go around, a hostess could choose an inferior piece for herself â the Popeâs nose (the back) or the wing tip â and insist that she preferred the *Knorpel* for its flavor.

A crust of bread or the heel of the loaf. Usually all that was left in the breadbasket after elder siblings had helped themselves. Also used of a small-portion diet or having just a taste of something, as in âOh, Iâll just have the *Knust.*â

pronunciation: NAH-yah, or if annoyed or exasperated: nah-YAH
definition: Roughly the equivalent of âWhateverâ or the more resigned âAh, what can you do about it?â In print and e-mail among Mueller descendants, it is usually misspelled *Nayah.*

Literally pine tar, pitch; bad luck. Unlike the lighter-weight *Kaplier,* above, *Pech* is the kind of trouble that sticks to a person like tar, a source of cosmic sorrow that worsens over time. Pech is getting a flat tire in the middle of nowhere, then discovering youâve left the jack in the garage ... and the spare is flat anyway. Pech is the Mueller land swindle. It remains a modern German idiom: *Pech haben,* to be down on oneâs luck, to get a bad break.

pronunciation: PFUH-ee â pronounced as a single-syllable word
definition: A catch-all evaluation of anything gross, ugly, reeking, filthy, repulsive, and well beyond indecent. Growing up, one heard it occasionally when something â speech, action or state of being â reached the existential equivalent of stepping in dog droppings. The source of the term was never clear, but it does appear in Heinrich Hoffmanâs *Streuwwelpeter* (see below), a work the parents, uncles and aunts had all encountered as children. Uttering a single, sharp *Pfui!* was like using the expletive *fooey* but far more potent.

To pique or nettle; [*adj.*] touchy (*pikiert Ã¼ber* is to be touchy about something). Used almost exclusively as a cognate in an English command directed at older children who were pestering younger siblings to the point of tears. It was pronounced PEEK-er: âDonât PEEK-erâ or âStop PEEK-er-ing!â

An outhouse, latrine. The etymology is unknown, but the term was widely used by Minnesota Muellers in the days before indoor plumbing, continuing until outdoor facilities no longer existed. One unsupported linguistic theory holds that the term was related to the militaryâs âpost exchange.â If someone approached or knocked on the door, a proper response for a male occupant was *âDer KÃ¶nig sitztâ* (the King is seated). *âDie KÃ¶nigin sitztâ* (the Queen is seated) would have been logical for women, but is undocumented.

Children who play creatively with water â splashing, squirting, pouring, adding soap suds â are said to be punschering, and all their activity and equipment â squirt guns, water balloons, hoses, sprinklers, wading pools â falls under the category of *Punscherei*. The word occurs in Cassellâs only in connection with a beverage and punchbowl, which is not what the Muellers meant. It is possible, though, that punschering derived from the creative assemblage of liquid refreshment.

Literally a rattletrap. Used of noisy old cars and worn-out pianos. *âSie trÃ¤gt aber einen Buick; ich einen Rumpelkastenâ* (But she drives a Buick and I drive a rattletrap) appears in a letter from Gerhard Mueller to his brother Herbert, politely declining an introduction to someone named Klara. The Muellers also used the term for children who got too wound up, who made lots of crashing and thumping noises while racing around upstairs. Kids who made lots of noise.

A sip, mouthful, swallow. Used often by the Muellers as a cognate in English, and most often in a diminutive sense: âThereâs just a *Schluck* of coffee leftâ or âIâll have just a *Schluck* of wine.â (See also *Schnippel* for the solid food equivalent.)

To eat noisily or to smack oneâs lips. Unlike *knabbering,* which was more about gnawing and crunching (see above), *schmatzing* was a noise associated with juicy or soft foods; loud slurping and repeated lip smacking.

To gabble (of geese) or quack (of ducks). Used of people, the word equated to muttering or murmuring but seemed not to cross the line to the full nastiness of gossip (which would have been *Quatsch*).

Chatterbox, a loquacious or gabby person, literally a gabbling goose. The third grader in the second row who cannot stop talking. Of kids, an excited motormouth. Of adults, incessant muttering, sometimes with the barest hint of gossip.

A banquet or feast; [*v.i.*] to feast or dine. *Sollen wir ihm einen Schmaus geben* would mean âShould we give him a testimonial banquet?â The Muellers â and especially the Nickels â used this grandiose word in a fully ironic sense: a snack, a little something, usually cheese, herring, *Stollen, Wurst, Schinckenbrot,* whatever delicacies were on hand. It was often initiated with the oxymoron, âShall we have a little *Schmaus?*â Germans donât do little *SchmÃ¤use.*

A scrap, shred, bit, chip or slice; [*v.t.*] to cut up, shred or slice. The solid food equivalent of a *Schluck* (sip, swallow), often misspelled as *Schnibbel.* A very small scrap of food, the trimmings of a roast after cutting it up, a sliver of cheese. Also used of a small slip of paper or wood shaving. Often used as a cognate as in âWonât you have just a *Schnippel* of ham?â

A childrenâs lap-bouncing rhyme, learned through countless happy, giggling repetitions with German-speaking uncles, aunts, parents and grandparents. It was passed down aurally, which accounts for infinitely varied misspellings. (Mueller descendants usually said âjuchelt.â) Although the Mueller version used only the first three lines, the verse remains a classic German childrenâs song, a [*Kniereiter zum Mitsingen:*](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eeTY4zivQEo)
Â Â *So fahren die Damen* [âThis is the way the ladies rideâ â a gentle, rhythmic bouncing]
Â Â *So reiten die Herren* [âThis is the way the gentlemen rideâ â stronger, faster bouncing]
Â Â *So ruckelt der Bauer* [âThis is the way the farmer ridesâ â bumpy, hilarious bouncing]
Â Â *Zum Tor hinaus* [âWhen they pass through the village gateâ â omitted in the spoken Mueller version]

Originally a culinary term having to do with lard or garnish and, in some German dialects, with smoked meats (*Spickgans* is smoked goose; *Spickaal* is smoked eel). In idiomatic usage, money changes hands: greasing someoneâs palms (*ihn spicken*) or lining oneâs pockets (*den Beutel spicken*). In Mueller and Nickel circles it always denoted paying for something â âIâll *spick* for the pizzaâ or *spicking* a kid to mow the lawn. Uncle Gerhard would visit Oak Park occasionally on a one-way ticket, confident that either of his sisters â most often Frieda, a softer touch â would *spick* him back home to Height of Land. He would put the *Spickung* in his pocket.

An intimate conversation usually late into the evening addressing numerous world problems, political, personal or theological issues, and catching up on who has been doing what and where. Not a true German noun, but a coinage from the verb *sprechen*, to speak. Sprechers were a naturally occurring part of a visit home or visits to siblings, relatives or friends.

The Muellers used this in its current figurative sense, *einen Stich haben* â to have a screw loose about something, to be touched, cracked, off oneâs rocker; an obsession, almost a superstition. âJimmy had a *Stich* about catching perch.â
A foundation or endowment; [*v.t.*] to endow, donate, make a present of. A glorified way of describing a contribution, as in âWhoâll *stift* for a six-pack of Grain Belt?â

Literally âSlovenly Peter,â a book of cautionary tales for children by Dr. Heinrich Hoffmann, also the title of the main cautionary tale in the book. Described by the author as *âlustige Geschichten und drollige Bilder fÃ¼r Kinder von 3â6 Jahrenâ* (amusing stories and comical pictures for children from 3 to 6 years of age), *Streuwwelpeter* was the gruesome stuff of nightmares. In one story, a little boy, forbidden to suck his thumb while his mother was out on an errand, starts the *Daumenlutschen* (thumb sucking) as soon as she leaves. A tailor dashes in with a large scissors and chops off the childâs thumbs. A girl plays with matches and burns to death. A boy who refuses to eat his soup wastes away and dies. Slovenly Peter will not comb his hair or trim his nails and so becomes a social outcast. Third-generation Mueller parents were skittish about *Streuwwelpeter* even in English; some of them kept it away from their children.

A genteel flatulence associated with a ladies tea party and proper manners. Possibly a Hedder-Mueller coinage from German Chicago in the 1890s. *Die Leisen die da schleichen, vor denen muÃt du weichen.*
To become lost, confused, annoyed. Often used when the driver of a car got hopelessly lost (âHe *verbeistert* himself in Owatonaâ or the intransitive âHe got *verbiestert* in Mankato.â)
To dribble soup or spill food on oneâs tie, shirt or blouse, make a mess of something, splatter. Often used as a reflexive verb (*âUnd jetzt habe ich mich verkleckst.â*)

Confused, dotty, afflicted with senile dementia. In polite conversation, an elderly person with dementia was said to be âconfusedâ or âforgetful.â In plain talk, *verrappelt* was a term for late-stage Alzheimers. The word is not in Cassellâs, but the noun *Rappel* (fit of madness) and the phrase *er hat einen Rappel* (he is off his rocker) are included.

Crazy, mad, insane, manic, out of oneâs mind. Unlike *verrappelt*, a more personal, even affectionate term for senility, *verrÃ¼ckt* carried serious, sometimes dangerous overtones of severe mental illness.

Relations, relatives, consanguinity. Used frequently by Muellers, who had a strong sense of kinship among them (âWe had the whole *Verwandtschaft* here for dinner.â) The term is also used in chemistry to denote an affinity between certain elements to form compounds.

âWhat will the people say?â This was a constant low-grade concern for the minister and the ministerâs family, particularly his wife, all of whom lived in a fishbowl. It wasnât just the congregation (which would have been *die Gemeinde*), but also the larger community of small-town and rural gossips, who expected a ministerâs household and family to run perfectly, have well-behaved children, and never to show the slightest hint of imperfection. It was a powerful incentive for children to stay on the straight and narrow.
